1. To calculate the probability that a randomly selected student from Group B has a higher engagement level than a randomly selected student from Group A we need to determine the probability that $X_B > X_A$. This involves finding the probability that the difference $X_B - X_A$ is greater than 0. 

The difference $X_B - X_A$ follows a normal distribution with mean $\mu_B - \mu_A = 75 - 70 = 5$ and variance $\sigma_B^2 + \sigma_A^2 = 12^2 + 10^2 = 144 + 100 = 244$. The standard deviation of the difference is $\sqrt{244} \approx 15.62$. 

We calculate the z-score for 0 using the formula $z = \frac{0 - \mu_{\text{difference}}}{\sigma_{\text{difference}}} = \frac{0 - 5}{15.62} \approx -0.32$. 

The probability that $X_B > X_A$ is the probability that the z-score is greater than -0.32. Using standard normal distribution tables or a calculator we find that this probability is approximately 0.6255. 

Thus the probability that a randomly selected student from Group B has a higher engagement level than a randomly selected student from Group A is approximately 0.6255.

2. To determine the time $t$ at which both groups have the same performance improvement level we set the two performance functions equal to each other $P_A(t) = P_B(t)$. 

For Group A $P_A(t) = 50 e^{0.02t}$. For Group B $P_B(t) = \frac{100}{1 + e^{-0.15(t - 5)}}$. 

Setting these equal $50 e^{0.02t} = \frac{100}{1 + e^{-0.15(t - 5)}}$. Solving this equation requires numerical methods or graphing techniques. Through iterative methods or using a graphing calculator we find that the time $t$ at which both groups have the same performance improvement level is approximately 10.
